key_insight: A drilled-to-bossed joint geometry combined with tuned DDFW parameters substantially improves AA2024-T351 weld strength, while deep learning can predict tensile performance from process settings.
engineering_relevance: The study offers a practical path to stronger lightweight aluminum joints for aerospace and automotive structures by linking joint design, process optimization, and AI-based prediction.
method: Used an L18 orthogonal array to vary friction pressure, forging pressure, spindle speed, joint geometry, and friction time.
Compared conventional flat-to-flat joints with a drilled-to-bossed geometry inspired by mortise-and-tenon interlocking.
Trained deep learning predictors including an ensemble residual network, compact attention network, and adaptive multiscale network to estimate tensile strength.
evidence_signal: The best tested condition reached 538 MPa tensile strength and 325 MPa torsional strength, and the ensemble residual network achieved the strongest prediction accuracy with R² ≈ 0.81.
watch_item: The reported AI prediction performance is moderate rather than high, so broader validation across more weld cases would be needed before deployment.
paper_title: Enhancing weld performance of AA2024-T351 using drilled-to-bossed geometry and deep learning prediction in direct drive friction welding
